sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Angela, presents with a history of recurrent kidney stones, with this being her seventh or eighth episode.
She reports that the current stone has passed, with no blood in her urine or pain for the past three days.
She has a past medical history of hypertension and diabetes, for which she is on 2.5mg of norvasc and 500mg of metformin daily.
She denies any chest pain or belly pain.
She has a history of fast food and high-sugar intake, which she struggles to control.
Her blood sugar levels have been inconsistent, with her last A1c being 6.7.
She has a systolic ejection murmur, which has not worsened since her previous visit.
She reports insomnia, which she has tried to manage with meditation apps and has considered using melatonin.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure was 150/90 mmHg upon arrival.
Physical exam: Lungs were clear bilaterally, with no crackles heard.
Heart exam revealed a grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur.
Abdominal exam showed no tenderness to palpation or the CVA on the right side.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has a history of recurrent kidney stones, which appear to have passed this time.
She has hypertension, which is currently well-controlled on 2.5mg of norvasc.
She has diabetes, with a recent A1c of 6.7.
She has a systolic ejection murmur.
The patient also has insomnia.
Problem list: Recurrent kidney stones; Hypertension; Diabetes; Systolic ejection murmur; Insomnia.
Plan:
I prescribed 10mg of melatonin as needed for insomnia.
I referred her to a urologist to assess the cause of recurrent kidney stones.
I ordered a urinalysis and urine culture to ensure the kidney stone has cleared.
I recommended a consult with a nutritionist to help her control her diet and reduce her medication usage.
I instructed her to take her blood sugar levels before each meal to monitor her diabetes management.
I will see her in three months to follow up on her progress.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in three months to follow up on her progress.
